title: The Civil Rights Movement
description: Test your understanding of the major concepts and events of the Civil Rights Movement in the United States.

assessment_items.question_content:
When **Rosa Parks** was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama for refusing to give up her seat on a segregated bus, the black citizens of Montgomery responded by

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The famous lunch counter sit-ins happened more than four years later in 1960.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: boycotting the Montgomery bus system en masse for 381 days, collapsing bus revenues and drawing national attention and the support of the federal government.
sitting at lunch counters that were reserved for whites only throughout the country, drawing media attention.
staging protests both outside the Supreme Court for 381 days, demanding laws be passed to integrate public seating accommodations.

assessment_items.question_content:
What facets of the **Montgomery Bus Boycott** would go on to serve as foundational elements of the Civil Rights Movement in the 1960s?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Civil Rights Movement rejected the idea of "separate but equal."

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: community organizing, nonviolence, and the intervention of the federal government
violent protest, community organizing, and the lack of support from the federal government
states’ rights, nonviolence, and “separate but equal” mentality

assessment_items.question_content:
**Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.** is best known for being

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The NAACP was founded in 1909, twenty years before Dr. King was born!

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a nonviolent advocate for economic, social, and political rights for people of color.
a civil rights leader who advocated the use of violent protest when necessary.
a founding member of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP).

assessment_items.question_content:
In **_Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka_**, the Supreme Court declared unanimously that

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The decision in *Brown v. Board* overturned the ruling in *Plessy v. Ferguson.*

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the doctrine of “separate but equal” was unconstitutional under the Equal Protection clause of the 14th Amendment.
the doctrine of “separate but equal” was constitutional as determined by the 1896 Supreme Court case *Plessy v. Ferguson.*
the decision to integrate schools would be left up to the individual states.

assessment_items.question_content:
The **Massive Resistance** campaign in the American South was waged by

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Black Americans were in favor of racial integration.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: white Americans who sought to prevent racial integration at all costs.
black Americans who did not want to see integration succeed due to fear of white retaliation.
black Americans who sought to further the cause of racial integration at all costs.

assessment_items.question_content:
What did the 1966 election of **Stokely Carmichael** to the head of the **SNCC** represent for that civil rights group?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Student National Coordinating Committee focused on grass-roots leadership, not top-down leadership.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A movement away from nonviolence and towards Black Power and black separatism
A stronger focus on top-down leadership with a charismatic figure at the helm
A stronger focus on advocating for school integration and less focus on public accommodations

assessment_items.question_content:
Beyond their religious faith, what contributed to **Malcolm X**, **Elijah Muhammad**, and **Muhammad Ali** changing the names they were born with?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Not all civil rights leaders changed their names.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A desire to decolonize and reclaim their identities, similarly to how African nations of the period changed their names when they sought independence from European colonial powers.
They wanted to have more easily recognizable names so that they could become more well-known media figures.
Changing one’s name was an important rite of passage as a civil rights leader.

assessment_items.question_content:
How did the **Civil Rights Act of 1964** fight racial discrimination in hiring practices?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The American Civil Liberties Union was founded in 1920, many years before the Civil Rights Act.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It established the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission to enforce anti-discrimination statutes.
It required companies to hire at least one employee whose job was to recruit and establish a diverse workforce.
It established the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) to defend individuals who came forward with discrimination claims.

assessment_items.question_content:
The **Civil Rights Act of 1964** had stalled in Congress in 1963, with hardline segregationists opposing the bill, but by 1964, it was law.  What was one of the factors that led to its passage?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: John F. Kennedy supported civil rights legislation.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The assassination of President Kennedy gave President Lyndon Johnson the political capital to enact Kennedy’s vision for the nation, which included the expansion of civil rights.

Increased US military presence in Vietnam caused segregationists to reevaluate their priorities; with so much danger abroad and a draft imminent, they focused their attention away from civil rights issues.
The assassination of President Kennedy gave President Lyndon Johnson the ability to support pro-civil rights legislation that Kennedy would have frowned upon.

assessment_items.question_content:
Poll taxes, literacy tests, and other barriers to black enfranchisement were made illegal with the passage of

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Civil Rights Act focused on eliminating discrimination based on race, color, or sex, but it did not directly prohibit most forms of voting discrimination.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Voting Rights Act of 1965.
The Civil Rights Act of 1964.
The Enfranchisement Act of 1965.

assessment_items.question_content:
How did the **Voting Rights Act of 1965** change the demographics of the two major American political parties?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Unlike African Americans during Reconstruction, African Americans did not join the Republican Party in the 1960s.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As African Americans joined the Democratic Party, many white southerners defected to the Republicans.
Democrats far outweighed Republicans in numbers as most African Americans joined the Democratic Party.
African Americans joined the Republican Party; most white Southerners stayed in the Democratic Party.

assessment_items.question_content:
What were some of the demands of the Black Panther Party, as laid out in their **Ten-Point Program** of 1966?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Segregated accommodations were officially outlawed in 1964 with the Civil Rights Act.

**Solution:**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Equitable employment, an end to police brutality, and exemption from military service for black men.
Free college education, fair trials for all black people in jail, and an end to segregated accommodations.
Free housing, and end to police brutality, and the establishment of a minimum wage.
